The core issue is learning expression/pose from RGB, where these factors are inherently intertwined.
AI.method: Loki encodes driver expression and head pose using a parametric face model with identity-orthogonal axes, rasterizes them into spatial maps for the diffusion backbone, and injects identity separately via lightweight key-value injection. Cross-ID reenactment is done by simple coefficient substitution.
AI.result: ~43% fewer inference parameters than leading diffusion baselines, trained on 1496x fewer video samples, and leads/co-leads on metrics directly measuring pose and expression fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Moving from RGB to parametric representation for conditioning simplifies portrait animation, reduces resource requirements dramatically, and achieves better disentanglement and cross-ID performance.

title: Single View Seafloor Recovery from Imaging Sonar via Differentiable Rendering
summary:
Sonar is often the only modality suitable for high-resolution imaging underwater due to light attenuation and turbidity. Forward-looking imaging sonar provides measurements over range and horizontal angle but collapses vertical structure into a flat image, creating ambiguities that make 3D recovery challenging. A common use case for imaging sonar is underwater terrain mapping (bathymetry), yet current methods require many views, expensive multi-sensor setups, or significant training data, which limits use and adaptability to new environments.
  We present a training-free method that recovers bathymetry from a single sonar image in under 30 seconds via differentiable rendering, conditioned on a known seafloor tilt. To our knowledge, this is the first differentiable rendering approach for single-view height recovery in sonar. Our method implements differentiable sonar ray tracing and optimizes an explicit height field to reproduce the target image. On synthetic datasets, our approach outperforms a supervised CNN under distribution shift and remains close on rough terrain, while the CNN wins in-distribution. By modeling physically grounded priors of the sonar process, our method adapts across sensor configurations and environments without training data.

AI.tldr: A training-free differentiable rendering method recovers bathymetry from a single sonar image in under 30 seconds by leveraging known seafloor tilt, outperforming supervised CNNs under distribution shift.
AI.motivation: Current underwater terrain mapping methods require many views, expensive multi-sensor setups, or extensive training data, limiting adaptability to new environments.
AI.method: Training-free approach using differentiable sonar ray tracing to optimize an explicit height field conditioned on known seafloor tilt, recovering bathymetry from a single image.
AI.result: Outperforms supervised CNN under distribution shift, remains competitive on rough terrain (CNN wins in-distribution). Adapts across sensor configurations without training data.
AI.conclusion: First differentiable rendering approach for single-view height recovery in sonar, offering fast, adaptable bathymetry estimation without training requirements.

title: Radiuma: A Unified Zero-Code Executable Graphical Workflow Generator for Reproducible and Shareable Medical Image Analysis and Machine Learning
summary: Medical image computing software is essential for identifying imaging biomarkers that can support diagnosis, prognosis, treatment planning, and clinical research. However, the lack of standardized, user-friendly, and reproducible software environments has limited the broader adoption of advanced medical image analysis workflows. We present Radiuma, a freely available modular platform designed to support reliable and reproducible medical image analysis across multiple modalities and file formats. Radiuma integrates image reading, visualization, registration, fusion, processing, segmentation, radiomics feature extraction, and machine learning modules for classification, regression, and clustering. Its modular design allows users to execute each component independently or connect modules through a visual workflow system, where the output of one step can be graphically passed to the next. This enables the creation of custom, executable, and reproducible multi-step pipelines without requiring extensive programming expertise. Results from each module can be inspected directly in the visualization window, providing immediate feedback on processing quality and workflow accuracy. Radiuma also supports saving and sharing customized workflows, promoting transparency, reusability, and consistency across collaborative studies. By combining flexibility, usability, and standardized analysis tools, Radiuma provides a practical environment for radiomics and machine learning research in clinical and translational settings. The platform is designed to be accessible to users with diverse expertise, including radiologists, physicists, clinicians, and data scientists.

AI.tldr: Radiuma is a free, modular platform for reproducible medical image analysis that integrates reading, visualization, registration, segmentation, radiomics, and machine learning modules via a visual workflow system, accessible to users without extensive programming expertise.
AI.motivation: The lack of standardized, user-friendly, and reproducible software environments has limited broader adoption of advanced medical image analysis workflows in clinical and translational research.
AI.method: The platform uses a modular design where each component (reading, visualization, registration, fusion, processing, segmentation, radiomics, ML) can run independently or be connected via a visual workflow system, allowing custom multi-step pipelines with graphical output passing.
AI.result: Users can inspect results from each module directly in the visualization window for immediate feedback, save/share customized workflows, and execute reproducible multi-step pipelines without extensive programming expertise.
AI.conclusion: Radiuma provides a practical, flexible, and accessible environment for radiomics and machine learning research, supporting diverse users including radiologists, physicists, clinicians, and data scientists in clinical and translational settings.

title: GIBLy: Improving 3D Semantic Segmentation through an Architecture-Agnostic Lightweight Geometric Inductive Bias Layer
summary: In 3D scene understanding, deep learning models rely on large models and extensive training to capture basic geometric structures that are present in the 3D data. However, existing methods lack explicit mechanisms to incorporate geometric information, such as learnable primitive shapes, often necessitating large models and more training data which in turn increases cost and can limit generalization. We introduce GIBLy, a lightweight geometric inductive bias layer that integrates learnable geometric priors into 3D segmentation pipelines. GIBLy enhances existing architectures -- whether MLP-based, convolution-based, or transformer-based -- by providing features aligned with simple geometric shapes (and thus human-interpretable) that improve segmentation performance with minimal computational overhead. We validate our approach across multiple 3D semantic segmentation benchmarks, demonstrating consistent performance gains, including up to +11.5% mIoU on TS40K with PTV3, while adding only 58K extra parameters. Our results highlight the benefit of explicitly encoding geometric structure to support accurate and efficient 3D scene understanding, with a lightweight add-on layer

AI.tldr: GIBLy is a lightweight geometric inductive bias layer that boosts 3D segmentation by integrating learnable primitive shape priors into existing architectures, achieving up to +11.5% mIoU with minimal parameter overhead (58K).
AI.motivation: Existing 3D deep learning models lack explicit mechanisms to incorporate geometric information (e.g., primitive shapes), requiring large models and extensive training data, which increases cost and limits generalization.
AI.method: GIBLy is a lightweight, plug-and-play layer that introduces learnable geometric priors (simple shape features) into 3D segmentation pipelines, compatible with MLP, convolution, or transformer architectures.
AI.result: Achieves consistent performance gains across multiple 3D semantic segmentation benchmarks, including up to +11.5% mIoU on TS40K with PTV3, while adding only 58K extra parameters.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly encoding geometric structure via a lightweight add-on layer enables more accurate and efficient 3D scene understanding, providing human-interpretable features without heavy computational costs.

title: CRISP -- Clustering-Based Redundancy-Reduced Instance Sampling for Pathology Case Representation and Retrieval
summary: Digital pathology archives increasingly contain multiple whole-slide images (WSIs) per case, capturing spatially distinct tumour regions and reflecting intrinsic morphological heterogeneity. However, most existing approaches rely on a single pathologist-selected slide, thereby discarding potentially informative evidence distributed across the remaining WSIs. To date, no autonomous framework has been proposed for comprehensive multi-WSI case processing. Here, we present an unsupervised framework for case-level analysis that integrates information from all available slides within a case. Rather than relying on a single designated slide, the proposed approach constructs case-level representations by selectively distilling informative patches across WSIs. We introduce Clustering-Based Redundancy-Reduced Instance Sampling for Pathology (CRISP), a two-stage framework that first reduces redundancy within individual WSIs and subsequently applies clustering-based sampling to select a compact yet representative set of patches for the entire case. The resulting patch set captures case-level heterogeneity while avoiding exhaustive processing of gigapixel images, and directly serves as a retrieval index. Using two Mayo Clinic breast cancer datasets for diagnosis and treatment planning, we demonstrate that CRISP consistently matches or surpasses the current standard practice of combined model and pathologist slide selection for patient/case search and retrieval. By automating case-level processing and eliminating subjective WSI selection, CRISP potentially enables the exploitation of clinically relevant information distributed across multiple WSIs that is currently overlooked.

AI.tldr: CRISP is an unsupervised framework that aggregates information from multiple whole-slide images per case by clustering-based patch sampling, achieving case-level retrieval performance matching or exceeding pathologist-guided single-slide selection on breast cancer datasets.
AI.motivation: Current digital pathology methods process only a single pathologist-selected slide per case, discarding diagnostically relevant information from other WSIs. No autonomous multi-WSI framework exists.
AI.method: CRISP (Clustering-Based Redundancy-Reduced Instance Sampling for Pathology) is a two-stage unsupervised framework: (1) reduces redundancy within individual WSIs via clustering, then (2) applies clustering-based sampling across WSIs to select a compact yet representative patch set for the entire case.
AI.result: On two Mayo Clinic breast cancer datasets, CRISP consistently matches or outperforms the standard practice of combined model and pathologist slide selection for patient/case search and retrieval.
AI.conclusion: Automated case-level processing eliminates subjective WSI selection and can exploit clinically relevant information distributed across multiple slides that is currently overlooked.

title: Plume Segmentation from MethaneSAT with Cross-Sensor Transfer Learning and Physics-Informed Postprocessing
summary: Automated detection and masking of individual methane plumes from satellite imagery is important for operational emission attribution and quantification. We present a machine learning framework for plume detection from MethaneSAT retrieved column-averaged dry-air mole fractions of methane. We address two core challenges: the scarcity of labeled MethaneSAT data and the need for inference reliability across diverse atmospheric and surface conditions. We first demonstrate that Mask R-CNN with a ResNet-50 backbone outperforms U-Net semantic segmentation on both MethaneAIR (an airborne version of MethaneSAT) and MethaneSAT data, with pixel-level F1 score gains of 10.49 and 5.48 respectively. To address MethaneSAT data scarcity, we evaluate three cross-sensor transfer strategies leveraging MethaneAIR flights and synthetic plumes. Mask R-CNN with ResNet-50 fine-tuned from MethaneAIR pre-trained weights is the most effective strategy, achieving instance-level precision of 0.60 and a near-perfect recall of 0.98 at the baseline operating point. A physics-informed post-processing pipeline converts detections into two operationally distinct modes. The first is a high-sensitivity mode that applies morphological filtering and proximity-based merging for comprehensive emission screening, achieving precision of 0.71 and recall of 0.94. The second is a high-precision mode that additionally applies a distribution-based classifier for confident source attribution, achieving precision of 0.92 and recall of 0.70. Manual review of detections classified as false positives against our wavelet-based ground truth labels reveals that a meaningful fraction of cases correspond to real methane enhancements excluded by conservative labeling criteria, indicating that precision values reported are lower bounds on true detection performance... Our data and code are available at: https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/FR959H

AI.tldr: This paper presents a machine learning framework using Mask R-CNN with ResNet-50 for automated detection of methane plumes from MethaneSAT satellite imagery, addressing data scarcity via cross-sensor transfer learning from MethaneAIR data and synthetic plumes, and offering two operational modes: high-sensitivity (precision 0.71, recall 0.94) and high-precision (precision 0.92, recall 0.70).
AI.motivation: Automated detection and masking of individual methane plumes from satellite imagery is critical for operational emission attribution and quantification, but faces challenges of scarce labeled MethaneSAT data and the need for reliable inference across diverse atmospheric and surface conditions.
AI.method: 1) Compared Mask R-CNN with ResNet-50 backbone vs. U-Net semantic segmentation; 2) Evaluated three cross-sensor transfer strategies (MethaneAIR pre-training, synthetic plumes) to address data scarcity; 3) Developed a physics-informed post-processing pipeline with two operational modes: high-sensitivity (morphological filtering + proximity merging) and high-precision (adding distribution-based classifier).
AI.result: Mask R-CNN with ResNet-50 outperformed U-Net by 10.49 (MethaneAIR) and 5.48 (MethaneSAT) in pixel-level F1. Best transfer strategy: fine-tuning from MethaneAIR pre-trained weights (precision 0.60, recall 0.98 at baseline). High-sensitivity mode: precision 0.71, recall 0.94. High-precision mode: precision 0.92, recall 0.70. Reported precision values are lower bounds on true performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables automated methane plume detection from MethaneSAT with transfer learning effectively addressing data scarcity, and the two operational modes allow flexible trade-offs between comprehensive screening and confident source attribution for real-world deployment.

title: Cross-Modal Action Recognition in Egocentric Video Using Mamba: Integrating RGB and Hand Skeleton Streams via CLS Token Fusion Strategies
summary: Egocentric action recognition is a challenging task due to erratic camera motion, frequent hand occlusion, and the difficulty of maintaining consistent visual representations over time. In this work, we propose a cross-modal architecture that combines RGB video and temporal hand skeleton data within a unified Mamba-based framework, exploiting the linear time complexity of State Space Models (SSMs). Our architecture consists of three components: a VideoMamba module for visual feature extraction, a skeleton encoder built on a stack of Mamba blocks, and a fusion module that integrates both modalities into a single representation. A central contribution of this work is the design and evaluation of four Class (CLS) token mixing strategies for multimodal fusion: Naive, Average, Weighted and Context-based. These strategies differ in how the pretrained unimodal CLS tokens, which role is to act as information sinks concentrating learned representations, are leveraged to initialize the mixed CLS token used for final classification. We evaluate all strategies on the H2O dataset. Experimental results show that the Average strategy achieves the best performance, yielding gains of over 10% Top-1 accuracy in the Tiny configuration and 2% in the Small configuration over the VideoMamba baseline.

AI.tldr: Proposes a cross-modal Mamba-based architecture combining RGB video and hand skeleton data for egocentric action recognition, with four CLS token mixing strategies, achieving up to 10% Top-1 accuracy gain on H2O dataset.
AI.motivation: Egocentric action recognition is difficult due to erratic camera motion, hand occlusion, and unstable visual representations over time.
AI.method: A unified Mamba-based (SSM) architecture with VideoMamba for RGB, skeleton encoder, fusion module, and four CLS token mixing strategies (Naive, Average, Weighted, Context-based).
AI.result: Average strategy achieves best results: over 10% Top-1 gain in Tiny config and 2% in Small config over VideoMamba baseline on H2O dataset.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal fusion with skeleton data and the Average CLS token mixing strategy significantly improves egocentric action recognition performance.

title: ArtSplat: Feed-Forward Articulated 3D Gaussian Splatting from Sparse Multi-State Uncalibrated Views
summary: Articulated object reconstruction from sparse-view images is an ill-posed problem that requires simultaneous inference of geometry and underlying articulation structure. Existing methods for articulated object reconstruction based on NeRF and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) typically rely on dense views or strong priors (e.g., depth maps, joint types, predefined number of joints) and require costly per-object optimization. In this paper, we propose ArtSplat, the first feed-forward framework for articulated 3D Gaussian Splatting. It reconstructs both geometry and joint parameters from sparse multi-view images across multiple articulation states in a single forward pass. To address the challenges of single-pass articulated reconstruction, we introduce a per-pixel joint map representation that enables the integration of joint parameter estimation into the feed-forward pipeline. We further propose a Cross-State Attention (CSA) mechanism with state tokens, which effectively captures discrete motion across input states. Experiments on 68 articulated objects from PartNet-Mobility, including both single- and multi-joint configurations, demonstrate that ArtSplat achieves competitive performance in both geometry and joint estimation, while being over 400 times faster than baselines.

AI.tldr: ArtSplat is the first feed-forward framework for articulated 3D Gaussian Splatting that reconstructs geometry and joint parameters from sparse multi-view images across multiple articulation states, achieving 400x speedup over baselines.
AI.motivation: Articulated object reconstruction from sparse views is ill-posed; existing methods (NeRF/3DGS-based) require dense views, strong priors, and costly per-object optimization, motivating the need for a fast, feed-forward solution.
AI.method: Proposes (1) a per-pixel joint map representation to integrate joint parameter estimation into a feed-forward pipeline, and (2) a Cross-State Attention (CSA) mechanism with state tokens to capture discrete motion across input states.
AI.result: Evaluated on 68 articulated objects from PartNet-Mobility (single- and multi-joint configurations), achieving competitive geometry and joint estimation performance while being over 400 times faster than baselines.
AI.conclusion: ArtSplat demonstrates that feed-forward articulated 3DGS reconstruction is feasible and efficient, eliminating the need for dense views, strong priors, or per-object optimization.

title: CoDA: Color Distribution Probing for Efficient and Generalizable AI-Generated Image Detection
summary: AI-generated image detection faces a persistent trade-off between generalization and efficiency: lightweight artifact-based methods often degrade on unseen generators or domains, whereas more robust large-scale models are computationally expensive. Meanwhile, existing benchmarks mainly focus on cross-model evaluation in photorealistic settings, leaving cross-domain robustness underexplored. To address this gap, we introduce FakeForm, a large-scale benchmark with approximately 370,000 images across 62 diverse domains for both cross-model and cross-domain evaluation. Motivated by this broader setting, we revisit color-distribution probing as an efficient complementary cue for AI-generated image detection. We observe that, especially for photographic content, real photographs tend to exhibit smoother and more stable color patterns, whereas synthetic images often show characteristic color imbalances introduced by neural generation. Based on this observation, we propose CoDA, a compact 1.48M-parameter detector built on a Noise-Quantization Probe, together with a theoretical analysis linking probe responses to color non-uniformity. Experiments show that CoDA achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard benchmarks and the best results on the challenging cross-domain evaluation of FakeForm, while remaining highly competitive in cross-model photorealistic settings. These results suggest that persistent generative artifacts can provide a practical foundation for efficient and robust AI-generated image detection. The models and FakeForm benchmark will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FakeForm, a large-scale benchmark for cross-domain AI-generated image detection, and proposes CoDA, a compact 1.48M-parameter detector using color-distribution probing, achieving state-of-the-art results while balancing efficiency and robustness.
AI.motivation: Existing AI-generated image detection faces a trade-off between generalization (large models are robust but expensive) and efficiency (lightweight methods fail on unseen domains). Benchmarks also lack cross-domain evaluation, focusing mostly on cross-model photorealistic settings.
AI.method: 1) FakeForm benchmark: ~370K images across 62 diverse domains. 2) CoDA detector: 1.48M parameters, built on a Noise-Quantization Probe analyzing color-distribution patterns, leveraging the observation that real photos have smoother/stable color patterns while synthetic images exhibit characteristic color imbalances.
AI.result: CoDA achieves SOTA on standard benchmarks and best results on FakeForm's cross-domain evaluation, while staying competitive in cross-model photorealistic settings.
AI.conclusion: Persistent generative artifacts (color non-uniformity) can serve as a practical foundation for efficient and robust AI-generated image detection.

title: Unified 3D Scene Understanding Through Physical World Modeling
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
summary: Understanding 3D scenes requires flexible combinations of visual reasoning tasks, including depth estimation, novel view synthesis, and object manipulation, all of which are essential for perception and interaction. Existing approaches have typically addressed these tasks in isolation, preventing them from sharing a common representation or transferring knowledge across tasks. A conceptually simpler but practically non-trivial alternative is to unify these diverse tasks into a single model, reducing different tasks from separate training objectives to merely different prompts and allowing for joint training across all datasets. In this work, we present a physical world model for unified 3D understanding and interaction (3WM), formulated as a probabilistic graphical model in which nodes represent multimodal scene elements such as RGB, optical flow, and camera pose. Diverse tasks emerge from different inference pathways through the graph: novel view synthesis from RGB and dense flow prompts, object manipulation from RGB and sparse flow prompts, and depth estimation from RGB and camera conditioning, all zero-shot without task-specific training. 3WM outperforms specialized baselines without the need for finetuning by offering precise controllability, strong geometric consistency, and robustness in real-world scenarios, achieving state-of-the-art performance on NVS and 3D object manipulation. Beyond predefined tasks, the model supports composable inference pathways, such as moving objects aside while navigating a 3D environment, enabling complex geometric reasoning. This demonstrates that a unified model can serve as a practical alternative to fragmented task-specific systems, taking a step towards a general-purpose visual world model.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes 3WM, a unified probabilistic graphical model for 3D scene understanding that handles multiple tasks (depth estimation, novel view synthesis, object manipulation) through different inference pathways from a single model, achieving state-of-the-art performance without task-specific training.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches address 3D visual reasoning tasks (depth estimation, novel view synthesis, object manipulation) in isolation, preventing shared representations and knowledge transfer across tasks. The authors aim to unify these diverse tasks into a single model where different tasks become merely different prompts.
AI.method: 3WM is formulated as a probabilistic graphical model where nodes represent multimodal scene elements (RGB, optical flow, camera pose). Different tasks emerge from different inference pathways through the graph - e.g., novel view synthesis from RGB+dense flow, object manipulation from RGB+sparse flow, depth estimation from RGB+camera conditioning.
AI.result: 3WM outperforms specialized baselines without finetuning, achieving state-of-the-art performance on novel view synthesis and 3D object manipulation, with precise controllability, strong geometric consistency, and robustness in real-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: A unified model can serve as a practical alternative to fragmented task-specific systems, supporting not only predefined tasks but also composable inference pathways for complex geometric reasoning, taking a step towards a general-purpose visual world model.

title: Causal Physics Steering in Video World Models via Concept Activation Vectors
comment: In proceedings of CVPR 2026 workshop on Video World Model
summary: Video world models learn representations of physical dynamics, but controlling their physical expectations at inference time remains an open problem. Recent interpretability work identified a Physics Emergence Zone (PEZ), a group of middle transformer layers in VideoMAE where physical plausibility is represented separately from other visual features. However, it remained unclear whether this structure could be used to directly control the model's physics reasoning. We present physics steering, a training-free method that uses the weight vector of a linear probe at a PEZ layer as a Concept Activation Vector (CAV) and injects it into hidden states during inference. This shifts the model's physical expectations without changing any model weights. On the IntPhys benchmark, this intervention reliably shifts the model's plausibility judgment in either direction, depending on the steering sign. The effect appears only when the intervention is applied within the Physics Emergence Zone, suggesting that the relevant physics representation is localized there. We further find that physics is encoded separately from motion direction, and that different intuitive physics principles occupy distinct directions within this representation space. Together, these results show that physical reasoning in VideoMAE is not only readable, but also directly steerable.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces physics steering, a training-free method that controls physical expectations in VideoMAE by injecting Concept Activation Vectors into the Physics Emergence Zone (PEZ) during inference, enabling directional shifts in plausibility judgments without modifying model weights.
AI.motivation: Video world models learn physical dynamics but controlling their physical expectations at inference time remains an unsolved problem; previous work identified that physics is represented in a localized region (PEZ) but it was unclear if this structure could be used to actively control the model's physics reasoning.
AI.method: The method (physics steering) uses a linear probe's weight vector at a PEZ layer as a Concept Activation Vector (CAV), injecting it into hidden states during inference without any training or weight changes.
AI.result: On IntPhys benchmark, the intervention reliably shifts plausibility judgments in either direction based on steering sign; effect is localized to the PEZ, physics is encoded separately from motion direction, and different physics principles occupy distinct directions in representation space.
AI.conclusion: Physical reasoning in VideoMAE is not only readable but also directly steerable, providing a training-free method to control the model's physical expectations.

title: ViViD-5K: Vineyard vision dataset for field-based berry detection and segmentation and grape cluster closure estimation
summary: Cluster closure, defined as the progressive filling of gaps between the berries in a grape bunch, is a key trait in vineyard management, impacting disease risk. However, traditional visual scoring methods are labor-intensive, subjective, and lack temporal resolution. Existing datasets rarely support fine-grained berry-level analysis, limiting the development of robust deep learning models. In this work, we present ViViD-5k, a large-scale in-field Vineyard Vision Dataset containing 5,000 images with dense annotations, including over 648,000 berry centroids and cluster segmentation masks spanning 13 grape varieties. Building on this dataset, we introduce GrapeSAM, a two-stage visual pipeline that combines point-based berry localization with prompt-based segmentation using Segment Anything, followed by transformer-based cluster segmentation. The pipeline enables automated, in-field estimation of cluster closure with minimal supervision. Quantitative results demonstrate strong segmentation and counting accuracy across diverse conditions, while visualizations confirm robustness on both in-domain and out-of-domain samples. This work provides a scalable and objective alternative to manual compactness scoring and supports high-throughput grape phenotyping with enhanced spatial detail.

AI.tldr: ViViD-5k is a large-scale vineyard dataset with 648,000+ berry annotations across 13 varieties, and GrapeSAM is a two-stage pipeline combining point-based berry localization and SAM-guided segmentation to automate cluster closure estimation in the field.
AI.motivation: Traditional visual scoring of grape cluster closure is labor-intensive, subjective, and lacks temporal resolution. Existing datasets lack fine-grained berry-level annotations, hindering deep learning model development.
AI.method: The authors created ViViD-5k (5,000 images, 648,000+ berry centroids, cluster masks, 13 varieties). They propose GrapeSAM, a two-stage pipeline: (1) point-based berry localization, (2) prompt-based segmentation with SAM followed by transformer-based cluster segmentation.
AI.result: Strong segmentation and counting accuracy across diverse conditions. Robustness confirmed on both in-domain and out-of-domain samples via visualizations.
AI.conclusion: This work offers a scalable, objective alternative to manual compactness scoring, enabling high-throughput grape phenotyping with enhanced spatial detail.

title: SparseWorld: Enhancing End-to-End Autonomous Driving via World Models with Sparse Scene Representation
summary: Recently, world models have made significant progress in enhancing end-to-end driving systems through both future situation forecasting and improved scene understanding. However, existing driving world models are typically built upon dense scene representations, causing high computational costs and redundant information. In this paper, we present SparseWorld, a lightweight world model that focuses on predicting only the critical layout of the scene, enabling efficient future forecasting for end-to-end driving systems. SparseWorld first performs autoregressive rollout to forecast future map elements and surrounding agents, enabling the model to learn how driving scenarios evolve over time. It then leverages these predicted futures to refine downstream motion prediction and trajectory planning. Specifically, we propose a Sparse Dreamer that anticipates future instances in the latent space through joint temporal and spatial attention. By interacting with predicted future instances, the motion planner captures more accurate motion patterns and generates more informed and safety-aware trajectories. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SparseWorld significantly reduces collision risk and achieves state-of-the-art performance on the open-loop planning metrics of the nuScenes dataset with a collision rate of 0.05\%. Moreover, it substantially outperforms the baseline method in closed-loop planning metrics on the Bench2Drive benchmark. Supplementary material is available at the project page: https://wryzju.github.io/SparseWorld/.

AI.tldr: SparseWorld is a lightweight driving world model that predicts only critical scene layout (map elements and agents) via autoregressive rollout, then uses these predictions to improve motion planning. It achieves 0.05% collision rate on nuScenes and outperforms baselines on Bench2Drive.
AI.motivation: Existing driving world models use dense scene representations, leading to high computational costs and redundant information. The authors aim to create a more efficient world model by focusing only on critical layout elements.
AI.method: SparseWorld uses (1) autoregressive rollout to forecast future map elements and surrounding agents, and (2) a Sparse Dreamer module that predicts future instances in latent space via joint temporal and spatial attention. The motion planner then interacts with these predicted future instances for better trajectory planning.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art open-loop planning on nuScenes with 0.05% collision rate, and substantially outperforms baseline in closed-loop planning on Bench2Drive benchmark.
AI.conclusion: SparseWorld demonstrates that focusing on sparse, critical scene representations can reduce computational cost while improving driving safety and planning performance, offering a more efficient alternative to dense world models.

title: Gaussian Rank-Based Neighborhood Degree for Graph Neural Networks in Image Classification
summary: The exponential growth of data has intensified the gap between the availability of unlabeled data and the high cost of manual annotation. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as a promising solution, as they exploit relational structures and learn from both labeled and unlabeled data, performing semi-supervised learning. A crucial component of many of these models is degree-based normalization, which influences message propagation but typically assumes uniform importance among neighboring nodes. In image classification, graphs are usually constructed from feature similarity, where treating all neighbors equally may overlook important variations in relevance. Motivated by this gap, we propose GRaNDe (Gaussian Rank-based Neighborhood Degree). This novel degree measure integrates neighborhood ranking with Gaussian distance weighting to better capture node importance. Experiments on five public image classification datasets show consistent accuracy improvements and competitive or superior results compared to state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: GRaNDe improves GNN-based semi-supervised image classification by replacing uniform degree-based normalization with a Gaussian rank-based neighborhood weighting that better captures node importance.
AI.motivation: The gap between abundant unlabeled data and costly manual annotation, plus the limitation of conventional degree-based normalization that treats all neighboring nodes equally, overlooking variations in importance.
AI.method: Proposes GRaNDe (Gaussian Rank-based Neighborhood Degree), integrating neighborhood ranking with Gaussian distance weighting for node importance estimation.
AI.result: Consistent accuracy improvements on five public image classification datasets, competitive or superior to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: GRaNDe effectively addresses neighbor importance variation in GNN-based image classification, yielding better semi-supervised learning performance.

title: SliceWorld: A Predictive and Controllable World-State Model for CT Report Generation
summary: CT report generation (CTRG) requires models to summarize three-dimensional anatomical context and pathological findings from hundreds of axial slices. Existing methods typically learn a direct image-to-text mapping, providing limited mechanisms for modeling how CT evidence evolves across slices or how reports respond to controlled changes in latent lesion-related factors. We propose SliceWorld, a CT-specific world-state framework that treats an axial CT scan as an ordered sequence along the z-axis. SliceWorld encodes prefix CT evidence into factor-aware latent states containing anatomy, lesion, and uncertainty components, and projects these states into world tokens used for multi-step future-slice feature prediction, lesion-factor intervention, and LLM-based report generation. The model is first pretrained on CT slice sequences with predictive, factor-aware, and counterfactual objectives, and is then fine-tuned on paired CT-report data. Experiments on M3D-Cap and CT-RATE show that SliceWorld improves natural language generation metrics and clinically oriented automatic evaluation. Further analyses demonstrate multi-horizon future-slice prediction, measurable factor alignment, reduced-slice robustness, and selective lesion-sensitive report modulation.

AI.tldr: SliceWorld proposes a world-state framework for CT report generation that models axial slices as ordered sequences, using factor-aware latent states (anatomy, lesion, uncertainty) for future-slice prediction, lesion-factor intervention, and LLM-based report generation, achieving improved performance on M3D-Cap and CT-RATE.
AI.motivation: Existing CT report generation methods learn direct image-to-text mapping but lack mechanisms to model how CT evidence evolves across slices or how reports respond to controlled changes in latent lesion-related factors.
AI.method: SliceWorld encodes prefix CT evidence into factor-aware latent states (anatomy, lesion, uncertainty), projects them into world tokens for multi-step future-slice feature prediction, lesion-factor intervention, and LLM-based report generation. Pretrained on CT slice sequences with predictive, factor-aware, and counterfactual objectives, then fine-tuned on paired CT-report data.
AI.result: Improves natural language generation metrics and clinically oriented automatic evaluation on M3D-Cap and CT-RATE. Demonstrates multi-horizon future-slice prediction, measurable factor alignment, reduced-slice robustness, and selective lesion-sensitive report modulation.
AI.conclusion: The world-state framework effectively models 3D anatomical context and pathological findings across CT slices, enabling controllable report generation and improved clinical evaluation outcomes.

title: VectorArk: Learning Practical Image Vectorization with Rounded Polygon Representation
summary: Recent vision-language model (VLM)-based approaches have achieved impressive results on image vectorization tasks. However, they are typically evaluated on synthetic benchmarks, where clean SVGs are rasterized at high resolution and then re-vectorized. As a result, these methods generalize poorly to real-world scenarios, such as images with unknown rasterization methods or those generated by text-to-image models. We introduce VectorArk, a new VLM-based model designed for robust and practical image vectorization. VectorArk employs a novel rounded polygon representation that simplifies the learning process while naturally producing smooth, visually appealing primitives. We also propose a degradation model that enhances robustness across diverse and imperfect inputs. Our experiments show that, in contrast to previous methods, VectorArk achieves superior geometric completeness and artifact suppression across multiple datasets, with comprehensive ablations validating the contribution of each component.

AI.tldr: VectorArk is a VLM-based image vectorization model that uses a novel rounded polygon representation and degradation model to achieve robust, practical vectorization on real-world images, outperforming prior methods that fail on imperfect inputs.
AI.motivation: Existing VLM-based vectorization methods are trained and evaluated on clean synthetic benchmarks (rasterizing then re-vectorizing SVGs), causing poor generalization to real-world scenarios like unknown rasterization methods or AI-generated images.
AI.method: (1) A novel rounded polygon representation that simplifies learning and produces smooth, visually appealing primitives. (2) A degradation model that enhances robustness across diverse and imperfect inputs.
AI.result: VectorArk achieves superior geometric completeness and artifact suppression across multiple datasets, unlike previous methods that fail on real-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed components (rounded polygon representation and degradation model) are validated via ablations and together enable robust, practical image vectorization for real-world use.

title: Dual Prototype-Conditioned Diffusion Model for Scalable Multi-Class Unsupervised Anomaly Detection in Large Category Spaces
summary: Multi-class anomaly detection aims to build unified models across diverse product categories. However, as the number of categories grows, its performance often degrades due to increasingly complex and heterogeneous normal distributions. To address this challenge, we propose DPDiff-AD, a Dual Prototype-conditioned Diffusion model for large-scale multi-class Anomaly Detection. DPDiff-AD models heterogeneous normal distributions through complementary local and global prototypes. Local prototypes capture representative fine-grained structural patterns via nearest-prototype aggregation, while global prototypes regulate holistic feature geometry through optimal transport regularization. Together, these dual-scale representations define a structured normality space. This space is refined through diffusion-based reconstruction conditioned on both local and global prototypes via prototype-aware attention. By jointly leveraging dual prototypes during generation, DPDiff-AD achieves precise normality modeling, preserves structured separability as category cardinality grows, and enables scalable anomaly discrimination. Extensive experiments across five benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness and scalability of DPDiff-AD. On the 160-category large-scale dataset, it improves image- and pixel-level AUROC by 5.3 and 2.9 points over the previous state-of-the-art method Dinomaly+, while maintaining stable performance as category cardinality increases.

AI.tldr: DPDiff-AD is a dual-prototype conditioned diffusion model for large-scale multi-class anomaly detection that uses local (fine-grained patterns) and global (holistic feature geometry) prototypes to model heterogeneous normal distributions, achieving SOTA results on 160-category benchmarks with 5.3 and 2.9 AUROC improvements.
AI.motivation: Multi-class anomaly detection performance degrades as category count grows due to increasingly complex and heterogeneous normal distributions, motivating a scalable unified modeling approach.
AI.method: DPDiff-AD uses dual prototypes: (1) local prototypes via nearest-prototype aggregation for fine-grained structural patterns, (2) global prototypes via optimal transport regularization for holistic feature geometry. These define a structured normality space refined by diffusion-based reconstruction with prototype-aware attention conditioning.
AI.result: On 160-category dataset: +5.3% image-level AUROC and +2.9% pixel-level AUROC over Dinomaly+, with stable performance as category count increases. Validated across five benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Dual-scale prototype conditioning enables scalable, precise normality modeling for large-scale multi-class anomaly detection, preserving structured separability as category cardinality grows.

title: Artiverse: A Diverse and Physically Grounded Dataset for Articulated Objects
summary: We present Artiverse, a diverse and physically grounded dataset of high-quality articulated 3D objects designed for realistic functional modeling and simulation. Artiverse contains 5.4K human-authored objects across a broad range of 88 categories, aggregated from multiple 3D static repositories. Objects are annotated with functional parts, interior structures, realistic kinematic relationships and articulated joints including multi-DoF joints, and physical attributes such as metric scale, material, and mass. We develop a semi-automated annotation pipeline that combines few-shot segmentation, geometric reasoning, and multi-stage human verification to achieve high-quality and efficient annotation, reducing manual annotation time by over 30%. We demonstrate the value of Artiverse on tasks of part mobility analysis, articulated object generation, and physics-based interaction. Artiverse provides a data resource to advance functional understanding for articulated objects.

AI.tldr: Artiverse is a large-scale dataset of 5.4K articulated 3D objects across 88 categories, annotated with functional parts, joints, physical attributes, and created via a semi-automated pipeline that reduces annotation time by 30%, supporting tasks like mobility analysis and physics-based interaction.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of diverse, physically grounded articulated 3D object datasets for realistic functional modeling and simulation.
AI.method: A semi-automated annotation pipeline combining few-shot segmentation, geometric reasoning, and multi-stage human verification to annotate objects with functional parts, kinematic relationships, multi-DoF joints, and physical attributes (scale, material, mass).
AI.result: 5.4K human-authored objects across 88 categories; 30% reduction in manual annotation time; demonstrated effectiveness on part mobility analysis, articulated object generation, and physics-based interaction tasks.
AI.conclusion: Artiverse provides a valuable data resource for advancing functional understanding and realistic simulation of articulated 3D objects.

title: Benchmarking Composed Image Retrieval for Applied Earth Observation
summary: Remote sensing composed image retrieval (RSCIR) enables search in large satellite image archives using composed queries that combine a reference image with a textual modifier. Although RSCIR offers a flexible interface for expressing targeted retrieval intent, the transferability of modern composition methods to Earth observation (EO) imagery and their relevance to operational EO workflows remain underexplored. We address this gap through a unified benchmark and an application-oriented study. First, we systematically adapt and evaluate representative composed image retrieval methods with six vision-language backbones on PatternCom under a standardized protocol, analyzing their behavior across backbones, composition strategies, and query types. Second, we introduce xView2-CIR, a change-centric dataset for disaster and damage monitoring, where retrieval is conditioned on scene identity and a target post-event state. Our results show that training-free composition methods provide strong and scalable baselines for EO retrieval, while change-centric retrieval presents different challenges from attribute-based retrieval, particularly due to the need to preserve scene identity. Overall, this study establishes a practical benchmark for RSCIR and positions composed retrieval as a complementary tool for remote sensing image retrieval, archive exploration, and change analysis. The dataset and code are available at https://github.com/billpsomas/rscir.

AI.tldr: This paper benchmarks composed image retrieval methods for remote sensing, introduces a change-centric dataset (xView2-CIR), and finds training-free methods work well while change retrieval poses unique challenges versus attribute-based retrieval.
AI.motivation: The transferability of modern composition methods to Earth observation imagery and their relevance to operational EO workflows remain underexplored.
AI.method: Systematically adapted and evaluated representative composed image retrieval methods with six vision-language backbones on PatternCom; introduced xView2-CIR, a change-centric dataset for disaster and damage monitoring.
AI.result: Training-free composition methods provide strong and scalable baselines for EO retrieval; change-centric retrieval presents different challenges from attribute-based retrieval, particularly due to the need to preserve scene identity.
AI.conclusion: This study establishes a practical benchmark for RSCIR and positions composed retrieval as a complementary tool for remote sensing image retrieval, archive exploration, and change analysis.

title: SILSM: A Sustainable Interactive Level Set Method for Progressive Refinement
summary: Interactive segmentation aims to precisely isolate target objects using sparse user guidance. However, traditional methods often suffer from heavy interaction burdens and parameter sensitivity, while deep learning approaches struggle with data dependency and iterative instability. Motivated by these limitations, we propose the Sustainable Interactive Level Set Method (SILSM). The proposed level set evolution equation incorporates interaction, regularization, and segmentation terms. Specifically, high-order regularization is employed to maintain numerical stability, and unlike traditional methods, we decouple user guidance into an independent interaction term to enable direct manual control over the zero-level set evolution. Furthermore, we develop a numerical algorithm tailored for multiple interactions, which facilitates dynamic refinement by effectively updating the segmentation results based on sequential user inputs. We theoretically demonstrate that the high-order term provides stronger regularization constraints than the conventional length term, while the interaction term ensures segmentation strictly within the user-selected region. Experimental results further demonstrate that the proposed method is robust to interactive inputs, achieves competitive performance at the first interaction, and supports stable multi-round interactions with progressively improved segmentation quality.

AI.tldr: SILSM integrates user interactions directly into a level set evolution equation with high-order regularization, enabling stable, multi-round interactive segmentation with progressively improved quality.
AI.motivation: Traditional interactive segmentation methods suffer from heavy interaction burdens and parameter sensitivity, while deep learning approaches struggle with data dependency and iterative instability.
AI.method: A level set evolution equation with three terms: interaction (decoupled user guidance), regularization (high-order for numerical stability), and segmentation. A numerical algorithm for multiple interactions enables dynamic refinement from sequential user inputs.
AI.result: The high-order term provides stronger regularization than conventional length term; interaction term ensures segmentation strictly within user-selected region. Method is robust to inputs, competitive at first interaction, and supports stable multi-round improvements.
AI.conclusion: SILSM reduces interaction burden and parameter sensitivity while avoiding deep learning's data dependency, offering stable progressive segmentation refinement through multiple user interactions.

title: EgoProx: Evaluating MLLMs on Egocentric 3D Proximity Reasoning Across a Cognitive Hierarchy
summary: Humans constantly reason about 3D proximity, the relations between their body and surrounding objects, to guide perception and action in daily life. Whether multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can perform such embodied 3D reasoning remains unclear. To this end, we introduce EgoProx, a benchmark for egocentric 3D proximity reasoning. We organize our tasks along a cognitive chain, covering intention, exploration, exploitation, and chain-of-actions reasoning. We also design an agent based data engine that produces diverse and consistent QA pairs at scale. We benchmark prevailing MLLMs on EgoProx and conduct additional analyses with dataset specific and task specific instruction tuning. We observe large cross-domain gains, indicating that current MLLMs contain some spatial knowledge; however, they still struggle to effectively leverage it for spatial reasoning VQA.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EgoProx, a benchmark for evaluating how well multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can reason about 3D proximity—the spatial relationships between a person's body and surrounding objects from an egocentric perspective. The benchmark is organized along a cognitive chain (intention, exploration, exploitation, chain-of-actions) and uses an agent-based data engine to generate diverse QA pairs. Experiments show MLLMs have some spatial knowledge but struggle to effectively use it for spatial reasoning VQA.
AI.motivation: Current MLLMs have not been thoroughly evaluated on embodied 3D proximity reasoning—the ability to understand spatial relationships between one's body and surrounding objects from a first-person perspective, which is fundamental to human perception and action.
AI.method: The authors create EgoProx, a benchmark organized along a cognitive chain (intention, exploration, exploitation, chain-of-actions reasoning). They design an agent-based data engine to produce diverse and consistent QA pairs at scale, then benchmark multiple MLLMs on this benchmark with additional dataset-specific and task-specific instruction tuning.
AI.result: Current MLLMs show some spatial knowledge (evidenced by cross-domain gains from instruction tuning), but they still struggle to effectively leverage that knowledge for spatial reasoning in visual question answering tasks.
AI.conclusion: While MLLMs possess some spatial understanding, there remains a significant gap in their ability to perform embodied 3D proximity reasoning, suggesting the need for further advancements in spatial reasoning capabilities.

title: Coarse-to-Fine Domain Incremental Learning with Attentive Distillation for Mining Footprint Segmentation in Multispectral Imagery
summary: Automatically mapping and segmenting global mining footprints using remote sensing and deep learning is critical for monitoring the socio-environmental risks and impacts of mining, yet its progress is hindered by the scarcity of fine-grained annotated data. Although large-scale datasets with coarse boundaries are widely available, leveraging them to improve fine-grained segmentation is challenging due to significant domain shift. To address this, we propose MineC2FNet, a coarse-to-fine domain incremental learning framework that exploits abundant coarse data to enhance fine-grained mining footprint segmentation. MineC2FNet adopts a teacher-student architecture with attentive distillation at both the feature and prediction levels, selectively transferring generalized knowledge from the coarse domain while enabling boundary refinement using limited fine-grained data (fine domain). We further introduce an expertly validated dataset of 219 images with precise boundary annotations across diverse geographies and commodities. Extensive experiments against state-of-the-art approaches, including domain adaptation and domain incremental learning methods, demonstrate that MineC2FNet achieves superior performance while effectively handling domain shift. The dataset and code are publicly available at https://github.com/risqiutama/MineC2FNet.

AI.tldr: MineC2FNet is a coarse-to-fine domain incremental learning framework that uses abundant coarse mining footprint data to improve fine-grained segmentation via teacher-student architecture with attentive distillation, achieving superior performance over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Automatically mapping global mining footprints is critical for monitoring socio-environmental impacts, but progress is hindered by scarcity of fine-grained annotated data despite availability of coarse boundary datasets, due to domain shift between coarse and fine domains.
AI.method: Proposes MineC2FNet, a coarse-to-fine domain incremental learning framework with teacher-student architecture and attentive distillation at both feature and prediction levels. It selectively transfers knowledge from coarse domain while enabling boundary refinement using limited fine-grained data. Also introduces an expertly validated dataset of 219 images with precise boundary annotations.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art approaches including domain adaptation and domain incremental learning methods in handling domain shift for fine-grained mining footprint segmentation.
AI.conclusion: MineC2FNet effectively leverages coarse data to enhance fine-grained segmentation while handling domain shift, with publicly available dataset and code enabling further research.

title: TempRet: Temporal Enhancement and Two-Stage Reranking for CVPR 2026 EPIC-KITCHENS-100 Multi-Instance Retrieval Challenge
comment: Technical Report for CVPR 2026 EPIC-KITCHENS-100 Multi-Instance Retrieval Challenge
summary: Video-text retrieval has witnessed remarkable progress driven by large-scale vision-language pretraining, yet most existing approaches inherit an implicit assumption from image-text retrieval: that visual semantics can be captured frame-by-frame. This assumption overlooks the temporal dynamics of egocentric videos. The EPIC-KITCHENS-100 Multi-Instance Retrieval (MIR) challenge further raises the bar by providing soft-label relevance matrices rather than binary labels, demanding models that can resolve graded semantic correspondences across modalities. In this report, we present our solution, termed TempRet, to the CVPR 2026 EPIC-KITCHENS-100 MIR challenge. Our approach builds upon a CLIP-based dual-encoder backbone and introduces two key components to address the temporal and cross-modal challenges. First, a temporal transformer operates exclusively on the video side, modeling inter-frame dependencies through learnable positional encodings and multi-head self-attention over frame-level CLIP features. Second, a two-stage reranking pipeline first retrieves Top-K candidates via the dual-encoder, then refines their scores using a cross-encoder equipped with an Image-Text Matching (ITM) head. The entire system is trained with Symmetric Multi-Similarity Loss to exploit the soft-label relevance matrices provided by the challenge. Our method achieves 67.97% average mAP and 82.92% average nDCG on the EK-100 MIR benchmark, demonstrating the effectiveness of temporal modeling and cross-modal refinement for egocentric video retrieval.

AI.tldr: TempRet is a CLIP-based dual-encoder solution for egocentric video-text retrieval, using a temporal transformer for video-side inter-frame modeling and a two-stage reranking pipeline with cross-encoder refinement, achieving 67.97% mAP and 82.92% nDCG on EPIC-KITCHENS-100 MIR benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing video-text retrieval methods inherit assumptions from image-text retrieval, treating visual semantics frame-by-frame and overlooking temporal dynamics of egocentric videos. The EPIC-KITCHENS-100 MIR challenge further demands handling soft-label graded relevance matrices rather than binary labels.
AI.method: TempRet builds on a CLIP-based dual-encoder backbone with two key components: (1) a temporal transformer on the video side using learnable positional encodings and multi-head self-attention over frame-level CLIP features to model inter-frame dependencies; (2) a two-stage reranking pipeline that retrieves Top-K candidates via dual-encoder then refines scores using a cross-encoder with ITM head. Trained with Symmetric Multi-Similarity Loss to exploit soft-label relevance.
AI.result: Achieves 67.97% average mAP and 82.92% average nDCG on the EK-100 MIR benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The temporal modeling of inter-frame dependencies and cross-modal refinement via two-stage reranking are effective for egocentric video retrieval, demonstrating superior performance on the EPIC-KITCHENS-100 MIR challenge.

title: Robust Fuzzy Multi-view Learning under View Conflict
summary: Trusted multi-view classification aims to deliver reliable fusion for accurate predictions and has recently attracted substantial attention in both academia and industry. However, existing TMVC methods typically assume strict alignment across different views during both training and testing phases, which is often impractical in real-world scenarios. This limitation motivates us to revisit TMVC and extend it to a more challenging setting: how to mitigate the impact of view conflict (VC) during both training and inference. To tackle this setting, existing TMVC methods suffer from three critical limitations: underestimated uncertainty, misleading decisions, and overfitting to VC. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel Robust Fuzzy Multi-View Learning (R-FUML) framework grounded in Fuzzy Set Theory. Specifically, R-FUML models network outputs as fuzzy memberships to quantify category credibility and uses an entropy-based method for reliable multi-view fusion. To this end, we present a Robust Multi-view Fusion (RMF) strategy that accounts for both view-specific uncertainty and inter-view conflicts, thereby alleviating the adverse impacts of VC on decision-making. To identify and conquer VC during training, we further design a Robust Learning Against VC (RLVC) framework. RLVC isolates conflicting samples by leveraging neural networks' memory effects and then retrains the model by applying a penalty to these conflicting views. Extensive experiments across eight public datasets demonstrate that R-FUML consistently outperforms 15 state-of-the-art baselines in robustness and uncertainty estimation. The code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: R-FUML is a robust fuzzy multi-view classification framework that uses Fuzzy Set Theory to handle view conflicts (VC) during both training and inference, addressing limitations of existing TMVC methods like underestimated uncertainty, misleading decisions, and overfitting to VC. It outperforms 15 baselines on 8 datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing Trusted Multi-View Classification (TMVC) methods assume strict alignment across views in both training and testing, which is impractical. They suffer from three key limitations when facing view conflicts: underestimated uncertainty, misleading decisions, and overfitting to VC.
AI.method: R-FUML models network outputs as fuzzy memberships via Fuzzy Set Theory, uses entropy-based reliable multi-view fusion, proposes a Robust Multi-view Fusion (RMF) strategy handling view-specific uncertainty and inter-view conflicts, and designs a Robust Learning Against VC (RLVC) framework that isolates conflicting samples using neural networks' memory effects and retrains with penalties on conflicting views.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across eight public datasets show R-FUML consistently outperforms 15 state-of-the-art baselines in robustness and uncertainty estimation.
AI.conclusion: R-FUML effectively mitigates view conflicts in multi-view classification, advancing TMVC to handle realistic misaligned scenarios with superior robustness and uncertainty quantification.

title: OmniEgo-R$^2$: A Routed Reasoning Framework for the 1st Cross-Domain EgoCross Challenge at CVPR 2026
comment: Technical Report for the 1st Cross-Domain EgoCross Challenge at CVPR 2026
summary: The 1st Cross-Domain EgoCross Challenge at EgoVis, CVPR 2026 evaluates whether multimodal large language models can reason over egocentric videos across surgery, industry, extreme sports, and animal perspective. We achieved second place in both the Source-Limited and Open-Source tracks. In this report, we formulate EgoCross as a robust cross-domain embodied video reasoning problem rather than a simple multiple-choice visual question answering task. We identify three key challenges: (C1) temporal boundary ambiguity, where critical state transitions are sparsely sampled and often occur between frames; (C2) cross-domain semantic granularity mismatch, where the same capability requires different domain-specific visual grammar; and (C3) decision instability under close options, where long multimodal reasoning can select unsupported distractors or produce malformed outputs. To address them, we propose OmniEgo-R$^2$ (Omnidomain Egocentric Routed Reasoning), a unified routed reasoning pipeline consisting of temporal-evidence normalization, domain-agnostic capability routing, structured perception--dynamics--decision reasoning, boundary-aware option verification, and defensive answer calibration. OmniEgo-R$^2$ uses the Qwen3-VL-4B-SFT checkpoints on each EgoCross domain as the visual-language backbone, and wraps them with lightweight test-time reasoning and parsing programs. Our final submissions obtain 66.35% overall accuracy in the Source-Limited track and 66.77% in the Open-Source track, ranking second in both leaderboards.

AI.tldr: OmniEgo-R², a routed reasoning pipeline for cross-domain egocentric video understanding, achieves second place (66.35-66.77% accuracy) in the EgoCross Challenge by addressing temporal ambiguity, domain granularity mismatch, and decision instability through temporal-evidence normalization, capability routing, and structured verification.
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLMs struggle to reason over egocentric videos across diverse domains (surgery, industry, extreme sports, animal perspective) due to three key challenges: temporal boundary ambiguity (sparse state transitions between frames), cross-domain semantic granularity mismatch (same capability requiring different visual grammar), and decision instability under close options (long reasoning selecting unsupported distractors or malformed outputs).
AI.method: Proposes OmniEgo-R², a unified routed reasoning pipeline with five components: (1) temporal-evidence normalization, (2) domain-agnostic capability routing, (3) structured perception-dynamics-decision reasoning, (4) boundary-aware option verification, and (5) defensive answer calibration. Uses Qwen3-VL-4B-SFT checkpoints as visual-language backbone for each domain, wrapped with lightweight test-time reasoning and parsing programs.
AI.result: Achieved 66.35% overall accuracy in Source-Limited track and 66.77% in Open-Source track, ranking second in both leaderboards of the 1st Cross-Domain EgoCross Challenge at CVPR 2026.
AI.conclusion: Formulating EgoCross as a robust cross-domain embodied video reasoning problem (rather than simple VQA) and addressing the three specific challenges with structured routed reasoning yields competitive performance, suggesting that domain-agnostic capability routing with defensive verification is a promising direction for generalizable egocentric video understanding.

title: Appearance-Invariant Detection of Suggestive Motion via Laban Movement Descriptors on SMPL Skeletons
comment: 2 pages, 2 figures. Accepted as a Poster at SIGGRAPH 2026
summary: Content moderation in online multiplayer 3D virtual environments has recently been relegated to automated, AI-based pipelines. However, the field has mainly been involved in detection of illicit content in images, video, and audio, leaving blind spots in detection techniques for suggestive motion. We present a motion-only classification pipeline that detects suggestive and explicit movement from SMPL skeleton trajectories using Laban Movement Analysis (LMA) descriptors. On 20,514 motion fragments (17+ hours) spanning four ordinal tiers -- everyday, artistic, suggestive, explicit -- logistic regression over 110 LMA features achieves 57.3% four-way accuracy (2.3x chance), 72.1% three-way, and 78.7% binary SFW/NSFW. Confusion concentrates on adjacent tiers, confirming that classification errors are concentrated between adjacent tiers over non-adjacent ones. Moreover, different movement qualities dominate at each level of the taxonomy -- no single feature drives the classification, suggesting that the four-tier structure reflects genuinely distinct motion regimes.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a motion-only classification pipeline that detects suggestive and explicit movements from SMPL skeleton trajectories using Laban Movement Analysis (LMA) descriptors, achieving 57.3% four-way accuracy (2.3x chance) across four ordinal tiers of motion content.
AI.motivation: Current AI-based content moderation in 3D virtual environments focuses on detecting illicit content in images, video, and audio, leaving a blind spot for detecting suggestive motion patterns.
AI.method: The authors use a motion-only classification pipeline that extracts 110 Laban Movement Analysis (LMA) features from SMPL skeleton trajectories, then applies logistic regression for classification across four ordinal tiers: everyday, artistic, suggestive, explicit.
AI.result: On 20,514 motion fragments (17+ hours), the pipeline achieves 57.3% four-way accuracy (2.3x chance), 72.1% three-way, and 78.7% binary SFW/NSFW classification. Confusion is concentrated between adjacent tiers, and no single feature dominates classification.
AI.conclusion: The four-tier structure reflects genuinely distinct motion regimes, and classification errors are concentrated between adjacent tiers rather than non-adjacent ones, validating the ordinal taxonomy of motion content.

title: Med-R2: An Adversarial Benchmark for Evidence-Grounded Reasoning in Medical VLMs
summary: Vision-language models have demonstrated impressive capabilities in general medical visual question answering, yet due to limited interpretability, it remains unclear whether their predictions reflect evidence-grounded clinical reasoning or reliance on spurious priors. We introduce Med-R2 Bench, a hierarchical benchmark aligned with the clinical workflow to evaluate adversarial robustness with visual grounding. We design stepwise QA tasks to assess whether reasoning chains are strictly grounded in visual evidence across the four clinical stages, and employ adversarial perturbations to test robustness against misleading cues. Med-R2 comprises 42,432 images, 31 task categories, and 110,406 QA pairs. Evaluation across 14 VLMs reveals a sequential performance degradation along the four-stage clinical workflow. Adversarial experiments show that models rely heavily on correct prompts to guess answers. Even when provided with explicit visual cues, the models struggle to accurately align textual descriptions. Finally, we demonstrate stepwise fine-tuning using our hierarchical data significantly improves reasoning robustness, highlighting its potential to drive future improvements in evidence-based medical AI.

AI.tldr: Med-R2 Bench is a hierarchical benchmark for evaluating adversarial robustness and visual grounding in medical VLMs, comprising 42,432 images and 110,406 QA pairs across 4 clinical stages. Experiments on 14 VLMs reveal sequential performance degradation, heavy reliance on correct prompts, and poor text-image alignment, with stepwise fine-tuning improving reasoning robustness.
AI.motivation: Existing medical VLMs show impressive performance in general VQA, but it is unclear whether their predictions are grounded in evidence-based clinical reasoning or rely on spurious priors/ shortcuts. The paper aims to address the lack of interpretability and robustness evaluation in medical vision-language models.
AI.method: The authors introduce Med-R2 Bench, a hierarchical benchmark aligned with clinical workflows, containing 4 clinical stages with stepwise QA tasks to evaluate visual grounding. They apply adversarial perturbations to test robustness against misleading cues. Evaluation is conducted across 14 VLMs, and stepwise fine-tuning is explored to improve reasoning robustness.
AI.result: Sequential performance degradation is observed along the 4-stage clinical workflow. Models heavily rely on correct prompts to guess answers and struggle to accurately align textual descriptions even when explicit visual cues are provided. Stepwise fine-tuning using hierarchical data significantly improves reasoning robustness.
AI.conclusion: Current medical VLMs lack robust evidence-grounded reasoning and are vulnerable to spurious priors. The proposed Med-R2 Bench provides a rigorous evaluation framework, and stepwise fine-tuning offers a promising direction for building more trustworthy evidence-based medical AI systems.

title: EgoAction: Egocentric Action Composition with Reliability-Aware Temporal Fusion for the EPIC-KITCHENS Action Detection Challenge at CVPR 2026
comment: Technical Report for CVPR 2026 EPIC-KITCHENS-100 Action Detection Challenge
summary: The EPIC-KITCHENS-100 Action Detection challenge evaluates whether a model can localize the start and end of each action in long untrimmed egocentric videos and assign the corresponding verb--noun action label. In this report, we formulate our submission as EgoAction (Egocentric Action Composition with Reliability-Aware Temporal Fusion), a unified decoupled detection and fusion pipeline. The pipeline uses EPIC-finetuned VideoMAE-L features, trains separate noun and verb temporal detectors with causal temporal modeling, composes action hypotheses from top noun--verb pairs, and introduces a confidence-adaptive boundary fusion rule at post-processing time. The key observation is that verb and noun streams often fail differently: verb scores are sensitive to motion transitions, whereas noun scores are sensitive to hand-object visibility and object clutter. A fixed arithmetic mean of their predicted boundaries can therefore amplify localization errors when one stream degenerates. We replace this hard-coded mean with Dynamic Weighted Fusion (DWF), which normalizes the maximum noun and verb classification confidences into proposal-wise boundary weights and linearly combines the two intervals. This lightweight tensor-only operator shifts boundary authority toward the more reliable stream while preserving the decoupled action scoring mechanism. Together with sliding-window inference, top-K noun--verb action composition, and class-wise Soft-NMS, EgoAction provides a compact and reproducible system for egocentric temporal action detection.

AI.tldr: EgoAction uses EPIC-finetuned VideoMAE-L features, separate verb/noun temporal detectors with causal modeling, and a Dynamic Weighted Fusion (DWF) rule that adaptively weights boundaries based on per-stream reliability.
AI.motivation: Verb and noun streams fail differently (motion transitions vs. object visibility/clutter), so fixed arithmetic mean of predicted boundaries amplifies errors when one stream degenerates.
AI.method: 1) EPIC-finetuned VideoMAE-L feature extraction, 2) separate noun/verb temporal detectors with causal modeling, 3) action composition from top noun-verb pairs, 4) DWF normalizes max classification confidences into proposal-wise boundary weights for linear combination, 5) sliding-window inference + class-wise Soft-NMS.
AI.result: The DWF rule replaces hard-coded mean boundary fusion, adaptively shifting boundary authority toward the more reliable stream.
AI.conclusion: EgoAction provides a compact, reproducible decoupled detection+fusion pipeline for egocentric temporal action detection.

title: EgoAdapt: A Multi-Scene Egocentric Adaptation Method for CVPR 2026 HD-EPIC VQA Challenge
comment: Technical Report for CVPR 2026 HD-EPIC VQA Challenge
summary: This technical report presents our solution, EgoAdapt (Egocentric Adaptation via Category, Calibration, and Consistency), to the CVPR 2026 HD-EPIC VQA challenge. HD-EPIC evaluates whether a vision-language model can reason over realistic first-person kitchen videos, where the evidence for an answer may be a short hand-object interaction, a long recipe trajectory, a spatial relation to a fixture, or a subtle gaze cue. The benchmark contains 26K multiple-choice questions across seven macro-categories: recipe, ingredient, nutrition, fine-grained action, 3D perception, object motion, and gaze. We observe that the main difficulty is not only model capacity, but also the mismatch between a single generic inference recipe and the heterogeneous temporal, spatial, and semantic structure of the benchmark. Our method, EgoAdapt, introduces three inference-time components: (1) category-conditioned routing with per-category prompts, frame budgets, and sampling rates; (2) calibrated option scoring that evaluates all candidate answers with letter-token likelihoods and generation agreement instead of relying only on direct generation; and (3) test-time consistency adaptation that aggregates predictions across option permutations and verification-style prompts for ambiguous cases. This design substantially improves over the available HD-EPIC baselines.

AI.tldr: EgoAdapt introduces three inference-time components (category-conditioned routing, calibrated option scoring, test-time consistency adaptation) to handle heterogeneous question types in the HD-EPIC VQA benchmark, significantly outperforming existing baselines.
AI.motivation: The main difficulty in HD-EPIC VQA is not just model capacity but the mismatch between a single generic inference recipe and the heterogeneous temporal, spatial, and semantic structure of questions spanning seven categories (recipe, ingredient, nutrition, fine-grained action, 3D perception, object motion, gaze).
AI.method: EgoAdapt introduces three inference-time components: (1) category-conditioned routing with per-category prompts, frame budgets, and sampling rates; (2) calibrated option scoring using letter-token likelihoods and generation agreement; (3) test-time consistency adaptation aggregating predictions across option permutations and verification-style prompts.
AI.result: The method substantially improves over available HD-EPIC baselines on the benchmark containing 26K multiple-choice questions across seven macro-categories.
AI.conclusion: Tailoring inference strategies (routing, scoring, consistency) per question category is an effective approach for handling diverse first-person video understanding tasks, rather than relying on a single generic inference pipeline.

title: FoodMonitor: Benchmarking MLLMs for Explainable Compliance Analysis
summary: As AI-powered compliance monitoring becomes increasingly important in public governance and industrial safety, the ability to provide verifiable evidence and traceable accountability signals is essential.
